questions.q: In API-led connectivity, which layer is responsible for unlocking data from core systems of record and insulating consumers from changes to those systems?
options: Experience API layer
Process API layer
System API layer
Orchestration API layer
questions.explanation: System APIs sit closest to core systems (databases, SaaS, legacy). They expose the underlying data in a canonical way and shield upstream consumers from changes to those systems. Process APIs orchestrate; Experience APIs tailor data for specific channels.

questions.q: A company wants to enable reuse and self-service of integration assets across lines of business, publish best practices, and measure asset consumption. According to MuleSoft, what organizational model should be established?
options: A Center of Excellence (CoE) that builds all integrations centrally
A Center for Enablement (C4E) that enables teams to build and consume reusable assets
An Integration Competency Center that approves every API design
An outsourced integration delivery team
questions.explanation: The C4E is a cross-functional enablement team: it ensures assets are productized, published, discoverable, and consumed, and it measures that consumption. Unlike a traditional CoE, it enables other teams rather than doing all delivery itself.

questions.q: Which HTTP method and characteristic combination is correct according to standard REST conventions?
options: GET requests include a request body describing the query
POST requests are idempotent and used to retrieve resources
PUT requests replace/update a resource at a known URI and are idempotent
DELETE requests must always include a request body
questions.explanation: PUT updates (or creates) a resource at a known URI, and repeating the same PUT yields the same result (idempotent). GET and DELETE conventionally carry no body, and POST (create/process) is not idempotent.

questions.q: Which Anypoint Platform component is used to discover, share, and reuse assets such as API specifications, connectors, and templates?
options: Runtime Manager
Anypoint Exchange
API Designer
Anypoint Monitoring
questions.explanation: Exchange is the marketplace/catalog of reusable assets. Runtime Manager deploys and manages apps, API Designer creates specs, and Anypoint Monitoring provides dashboards and alerts.

questions.q: What is the primary cause of the 'IT delivery gap' that MuleSoft's application network approach aims to close?
options: Businesses request fewer projects while IT capacity grows
The demand on IT grows much faster than IT's delivery capacity with point-to-point integration
Cloud providers limit the number of APIs a company can build
Developers refuse to document their code
questions.explanation: Business demands on IT grow rapidly, but custom point-to-point integration doesn't scale, so IT delivery capacity stays roughly flat. Reusable APIs in an application network let each project deliver faster than the last, closing the gap.

questions.q: A web storefront needs data aggregated from several process APIs, formatted specifically for its UI. In API-led connectivity, where should this channel-specific formatting logic live?
options: In the System API layer
In the Experience API layer
In the database stored procedures
In an API proxy policy
questions.explanation: Experience APIs reconfigure data for the needs of a specific channel or end user experience (web, mobile, partner). System APIs unlock core systems and Process APIs hold channel-agnostic business logic.

questions.q: In a RAML specification, what is the correct way to define a URI parameter for retrieving a single flight by its ID?
options: /flights?ID: get:
/flights/{ID}: with a nested get: method
/flights/:ID: with a nested get: method
/flights/[ID]: with a nested get: method
questions.explanation: RAML nests resources, and URI parameters are declared with curly braces: /flights/{ID}. The :ID syntax belongs to other frameworks (e.g. Express), and square brackets are not valid RAML.

questions.q: When should a query parameter be used instead of a URI parameter in an API design?
options: When identifying a specific, unique resource
When filtering or restricting the set of results returned from a collection
When sending sensitive credentials
When the value is required for the request to succeed
questions.explanation: URI parameters identify a specific resource (/flights/23); query parameters filter, sort, or paginate collections (/flights?destination=SFO). Required vs optional is orthogonal — query params can be required too, but resource identity belongs in the URI.

questions.q: An API design team wants consumers to try an API and give feedback before any implementation is built. What Anypoint Platform feature makes this possible?
options: The mocking service, exercised from the API console in Exchange or API Designer
Runtime Manager sandbox workers
The Mule debugger
API Manager SLA tiers
questions.explanation: The mocking service simulates the API from its specification and example data, so consumers can send real HTTP requests to a mock endpoint from the API console — a key part of the design-first lifecycle (design, simulate, get feedback, validate).

questions.q: In RAML, what is the purpose of a trait?
options: To define a reusable data type for request bodies
To define a reusable set of method-level properties, such as pagination query parameters, applied with 'is:'
To secure the API with OAuth
To split the RAML file into multiple physical files
questions.explanation: Traits capture reusable method-level patterns (query params, headers, responses) and are applied to methods with 'is: [traitName]'. resourceTypes do the same at resource level; types define data structures; !include splits files.

questions.q: A RAML spec defines: /flights: get: queryParameters: destination: enum: [SFO, LAX, CLE]. Which request is valid against this API?
options: GET /flights/destination/SFO
GET /flights?destination=SFO
POST /flights with body {"destination": "SFO"}
GET /flights?dest=SFO
questions.explanation: destination is defined as a query parameter of the GET /flights method, so it is passed as ?destination=SFO. The parameter name must match exactly, and it is not a URI path segment.

questions.q: Which RAML fragment type is used to define a reusable data structure that can be published to Exchange and referenced from multiple API specifications?
options: NamedExample fragment
DataType fragment
Trait fragment
Overlay fragment
questions.explanation: A DataType fragment defines a reusable type (e.g. AmericanFlight). NamedExample holds example instances, Traits hold method patterns, and Overlays/Extensions modify existing specs.

questions.q: What are the two main parts of a Mule message?
options: Payload and variables
Payload and attributes
Headers and body
Inbound properties and outbound properties
questions.explanation: A Mule message = payload (the data) + attributes (metadata such as HTTP query params and headers). Variables belong to the Mule event, alongside the message. Inbound/outbound properties were Mule 3 concepts.

questions.q: A flow receives an HTTP request with the query parameter ?code=SFO. Which DataWeave expression accesses its value?
options: payload.code
attributes.queryParams.code
vars.queryParams.code
message.inboundProperties.code
questions.explanation: HTTP metadata like query parameters, URI parameters, and headers is stored in the message attributes: attributes.queryParams.code. The payload holds the request body, and vars are only what you set yourself.

questions.q: What happens to the attributes of a Mule message when the event passes through an HTTP Request operation?
options: They are preserved unchanged
They are merged with the response headers
They are replaced by new attributes from the HTTP response (status code, headers, etc.)
They are copied into flow variables automatically
questions.explanation: Every connector operation that returns a message replaces the attributes with metadata about ITS result — after an HTTP Request, attributes contain the response status code and headers. Save anything you need first (e.g. in a variable).

questions.q: A developer needs to call an external API to get a currency rate but must keep the current payload intact for the next processor. What is the recommended approach?
options: Wrap the HTTP Request in a Try scope
Set the target parameter of the HTTP Request operation to store the response in a variable
Copy the payload into the attributes first
Use a subflow so payload changes are discarded
questions.explanation: The target (and target value) parameter stores the operation's output in a variable instead of overwriting the payload — the standard Mule 4 enrichment pattern.

questions.q: Which statement about flow variables (vars) is true?
options: They persist across an HTTP Request call to another Mule application
They are lost when the event passes through a Flow Reference
They persist for the current flow and through Flow References within the same application
They can only store String values
questions.explanation: Variables travel with the Mule event through Flow References (and back). They do NOT cross a transport boundary to another application — only the payload and any headers you explicitly set are transmitted. Variables can hold any type.

questions.q: In an event processor's field, what syntax embeds a DataWeave expression inline?
options: ${payload.name}
#[payload.name]
{{payload.name}}
%[payload.name]
questions.explanation: Inline DataWeave expressions use #[ ... ]. The ${ } syntax is for property placeholders resolved at startup, not runtime expressions.

questions.q: What is the difference between a subflow and a private flow?
options: A subflow has an event source; a private flow does not
A private flow has its own error handling scope; a subflow does not and inherits the caller's
A subflow can only be called once per application
There is no difference; the names are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Neither has an event source, but a private flow has its own processing strategy and error handling, while a subflow inherits everything from the calling flow (and is slightly more performant).

questions.q: When a Flow Reference calls another flow in the same application, what data does the called flow receive?
options: Only the payload
The payload and attributes but not variables
The entire Mule event: payload, attributes, and variables
A copy of the event; changes are discarded on return
questions.explanation: A Flow Reference passes the whole Mule event, and any changes made by the referenced flow (payload, variables) come back to the calling flow. This differs from crossing a transport boundary, where only the payload is sent.

questions.q: A developer wants database credentials to differ between dev and prod without changing code. What is the recommended Mule 4 approach?
options: Hardcode both sets and use a Choice router
Store values in YAML properties files (e.g. dev.yaml, prod.yaml) selected by a system property like -Denv=dev, referenced with ${db.user}
Store credentials in the flow name
Use DataWeave vars in every connector configuration
questions.explanation: Property placeholders externalize configuration. A common pattern is per-environment YAML files whose name is built from an env system property: <file>${env}.yaml</file>, referenced via ${db.user} in configs and p('db.user') in DataWeave.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Mule domain project?
options: To bundle multiple applications into one deployable JAR
To share global configurations (like an HTTP Listener config and port) among applications deployed to the same customer-hosted Mule runtime
To enable CloudHub applications to share vCores
To group APIs in Exchange
questions.explanation: Domains let apps on the same customer-hosted runtime share resources such as HTTP Listener configurations (port sharing) and connections. Domains are not supported on CloudHub, where each app runs on its own worker.

questions.q: In DataWeave, how do you read a value from a configuration properties file registered in the application?
options: vars.db_host
p('db.host')
properties.db.host
${db.host} inside the DataWeave body
questions.explanation: In DataWeave scripts, use the p() function: p('db.host'). The ${db.host} placeholder syntax works in component configuration fields, not inside DataWeave expressions.

questions.q: Why would a developer move connector configurations into a separate global elements XML file?
options: It is required for the application to compile
To improve readability and organize reusable configurations referenced by many flows
Global elements only work in a separate file
To encrypt the configurations
questions.explanation: Global configurations can live in any config file; keeping them in a dedicated file (e.g. global.xml) is a best practice for organization and reuse. It's a convention, not a requirement.

questions.q: When APIkit generates flows from a RAML specification, how does the APIkit Router determine which flow handles a request?
options: By the flow's position in the configuration file
By matching the request's method and resource path to the flow's name (e.g. get:\flights:api-config)
By reading a routing table in the pom.xml
By evaluating a Choice router inside the main flow
questions.explanation: APIkit routes by flow naming convention: the generated flow name encodes method, resource (and config name). Renaming these flows breaks routing.

questions.q: A request violates the API specification (a required query parameter is missing). What does the APIkit Router do by default?
options: Routes the request anyway and lets the implementation fail
Raises an APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST error, which the generated error handler maps to a 400 response
Returns a 500 response with no body
Silently drops the request
questions.explanation: The APIkit Router validates requests against the spec. Invalid requests raise APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST, and the scaffolded error handlers return the corresponding HTTP status (400).

questions.q: What does the anypoint platform automatically generate when a RAML API specification is published to Exchange, making the API easy to consume from Studio?
options: A SOAP WSDL
A REST Connector (via REST Connect) with one operation per method
A Java SDK
An APIkit error handler
questions.explanation: REST Connect generates a connector from the published spec. Developers add it to their project from Exchange and get typed operations instead of hand-configured HTTP Request calls.

questions.q: Inside an APIkit-generated implementation flow for GET /flights/{ID}, how is the ID value accessed?
options: payload.ID
attributes.uriParams.ID
vars.ID
attributes.queryParams.ID
questions.explanation: URI parameters extracted by the router are placed in attributes.uriParams. Query parameters go to attributes.queryParams.

questions.q: Which statement describes a benefit of generating an API interface with APIkit rather than building it manually?
options: APIkit removes the need for an HTTP Listener
APIkit guarantees zero-downtime deployments
The interface stays consistent with the specification, and request validation plus error handling scaffolding come for free
APIkit automatically writes the implementation logic for each resource
questions.explanation: APIkit scaffolds routing flows, validates requests against the spec, and generates standard error handlers. Business logic still has to be implemented by the developer.

questions.q: A developer regenerates APIkit flows after the RAML spec added a new resource. What happens to existing implementation flows?
options: They are deleted and must be rewritten
They are preserved; APIkit adds flows only for the new resource/method pairs
They are renamed with a version suffix
They are moved to a backup project
questions.explanation: Regenerating flows from an updated spec adds missing flows for new resources/methods while leaving existing implementation flows intact.

questions.q: In a Choice router with three 'when' routes and a default route, the event matches the conditions of both the first and second routes. What happens?
options: Both matching routes execute in parallel
Only the first matching route executes
The default route executes because the match is ambiguous
An error is raised
questions.explanation: The Choice router executes exactly one route: the FIRST whose expression evaluates true. If none match, the otherwise/default route runs.

questions.q: What payload does a Scatter-Gather return after all its routes complete successfully?
options: The payload of the last route only
An array of payloads in route order
An object with keys "0", "1", ... each containing that route's Mule message (payload and attributes)
The original request payload unchanged
questions.explanation: Scatter-Gather aggregates results into an object keyed by route index; each value holds that route's message. Developers typically follow it with DataWeave like flatten(payload pluck $.payload) to merge results.

questions.q: How does the Scatter-Gather router process its routes?
options: Sequentially, stopping at the first failure
Concurrently, sending a copy of the event to every route and waiting for all to complete
Round-robin, one route per incoming event
Only the fastest route's result is kept
questions.explanation: Scatter-Gather multicasts a copy of the event to all routes in parallel and waits for all of them. Round-robin describes the Round Robin router; 'first successful' describes First Successful.

questions.q: What happens when an 'Is number' validation component from the Validation module receives a non-numeric string?
options: It sets the payload to false
It throws a Mule error of type VALIDATION:INVALID_NUMBER
It logs a warning and continues
It converts the value to 0
questions.explanation: Validation components throw typed Mule errors on failure (VALIDATION namespace) rather than returning booleans, so failures can be handled by error handlers.

questions.q: A flow must route an order to exactly one of three handlers based on payload.orderType, with a fallback for unknown types. Which component fits best?
options: Scatter-Gather
Choice router with three when routes and a default route
For Each scope
First Successful router
questions.explanation: Conditional single-route dispatching with a fallback is exactly the Choice router's job. Scatter-Gather would send the order to ALL handlers.

questions.q: By default, what happens if one route inside a Scatter-Gather fails?
options: The other routes are cancelled and their results discarded silently
Scatter-Gather waits for all routes, then raises a composite routing error containing the failure(s)
The failed route is retried three times
The failure is ignored and the aggregate contains only successful routes
questions.explanation: All routes run; if any fail, a COMPOSITE_ROUTING error aggregating the failures is raised. Handling partial failures requires error handling inside the routes (e.g. Try scopes).

questions.q: An HTTP-triggered flow has an On Error Continue scope that matches the raised error. What HTTP response does the caller receive?
options: A 500 error response, because an error occurred
The flow's success response (200 by default), with the payload produced by the error handler
A 400 Bad Request
No response; the connection is closed
questions.explanation: On Error Continue handles the error and continues as if the flow succeeded, so the HTTP Listener returns its success response. On Error Propagate would return the error (500) response.

questions.q: Flow A calls Flow B via Flow Reference. Flow B raises an error that is handled by an On Error Propagate scope in Flow B. What happens in Flow A?
options: Flow A continues normally after the Flow Reference
The error propagates to Flow A, where its error handling (or the default handler) takes over; the rest of Flow A does not execute normally
Flow A restarts from the beginning
Flow A receives the error as its payload and continues
questions.explanation: On Error Propagate re-throws the error after running its processors, so the error surfaces at the Flow Reference in Flow A and is handled by Flow A's error handling.

questions.q: Which expression accesses the type of the error that was raised, inside an error handler?
options: payload.errorType
error.errorType
vars.error.type
attributes.error
questions.explanation: In an error handler, the error object exposes error.errorType, error.description, and error.cause. The payload remains whatever it was when the error occurred.

questions.q: A developer wants one error handler to be used by all flows in the application that do not define their own. What should they configure?
options: An On Error Continue in every flow
A Try scope around every flow
A global error handler referenced as the application's default error handler in the project configuration
An APIkit console flow
questions.explanation: Define a global error handler (Error Handler global element) and set it as the default error handler in the Mule configuration properties. It applies only to flows without their own handler — it is a fallback, not an addition.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Try scope?
options: To retry failed processors automatically
To handle errors at the level of one or more processors inside a flow, with its own error handling scopes
To run processors in parallel
To catch Java exceptions only, not Mule errors
questions.explanation: A Try scope wraps a set of processors and attaches error handling to just that section, enabling processor-level handling (e.g. continue after one risky call fails). It does not retry by itself — that's Until Successful.

questions.q: An error handler has two On Error scopes: the first matches type MULE:ANY, the second matches HTTP:NOT_FOUND. An HTTP:NOT_FOUND error is raised. Which scope handles it?
options: The second, because it is the most specific match
The first, because scopes are evaluated in order and MULE:ANY matches everything
Both scopes execute in order
Neither; the error propagates
questions.explanation: Error scopes are evaluated top to bottom and the FIRST match wins. Since all errors extend MULE:ANY, the first scope catches everything — specific handlers must be placed before general ones.

questions.q: Why would a developer configure error mapping on a connector operation?
options: To retry the operation with backoff
To convert a low-level error (e.g. HTTP:NOT_FOUND) into a custom, application-specific error type (e.g. APP:CUSTOMER_NOT_FOUND)
To suppress all errors from the operation
To log the error automatically
questions.explanation: Error mapping renames/reclassifies errors into custom namespaces so error handlers can react to business-meaningful types instead of transport-level ones.

questions.q:
Given payload = [{price: 10}, {price: 20}], what does this script return?

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload map (item, index) -> { p: item.price * 2 }
options: [{price: 20}, {price: 40}]
An error, because map requires $ syntax
questions.explanation: map transforms each array element with the lambda; named parameters (item, index) are equivalent to $ and $$. Each element becomes an object with key p and doubled price.

questions.q: Which DataWeave expression removes duplicate elements from an array of objects based on their 'id' field?
options: payload filter $.id
payload distinctBy $.id
payload groupBy $.id
payload orderBy $.id
questions.explanation: distinctBy returns unique elements based on the criteria expression. groupBy would return an object of arrays keyed by id; filter keeps elements matching a boolean.

questions.q: What must be true for a DataWeave script that outputs application/xml?
options: The body must produce a single root element
The body must be an array
All values must be strings
The script must import dw::xml
questions.explanation: XML documents require exactly one root element, so the top level of the script body must reduce to a single key. Multiple top-level keys or an array cause an error.

questions.q: Which expression converts the string "2024-03-15" into a Date and formats today's date as "15-03-2024" style output?
options: "2024-03-15" as Date and now() as String {format: "dd-MM-yyyy"}
toDate("2024-03-15") and format(now(), "dd-MM-yyyy")
Date("2024-03-15") and now().format("dd-MM-yyyy")
"2024-03-15" as String {format: Date}
questions.explanation: Type coercion uses the 'as' operator, optionally with format metadata: value as Date, and now() as String {format: "dd-MM-yyyy"}.

questions.q: Where should a reusable DataWeave function library be placed, and how is it used in a Transform Message component?
options: In a .dwl file under src/main/resources (e.g. modules/MyModule.dwl), imported with 'import modules::MyModule'
In pom.xml as a dependency
In the flow's error handler
In a Java class annotated @DataWeave
questions.explanation: Custom modules are .dwl files on the classpath (src/main/resources). Import them in the header: import modules::MyModule (then MyModule::myFun) or import * from modules::MyModule.

questions.q: Which DataWeave function calls another flow from inside a script and returns that flow's payload?
options: invoke()
lookup()
callFlow()
flowRef()
questions.explanation: lookup("flowName", payload) executes a flow (not a subflow) and returns its resulting payload. It should be used sparingly — errors in the called flow can be hard to handle.

questions.q: Given payload = [{"dest":"SFO","price":100},{"dest":"LAX","price":75},{"dest":"SFO","price":90}], what does 'payload groupBy $.dest' return?
options: An array sorted by dest
An object with keys "SFO" and "LAX", each containing an array of the matching objects
An array with duplicate destinations removed
The count of flights per destination
questions.explanation: groupBy returns an object whose keys are the criteria values and whose values are arrays of matching elements: {SFO: [...2 items...], LAX: [...1 item...]}.

questions.q: In a DataWeave script, where are 'var' and 'fun' declarations placed?
options: In the body, after the --- separator
In the header, before the --- separator
In a separate properties file
Inside #[ ] blocks only
questions.explanation: The header (above ---) holds directives: %dw 2.0, output, var, fun, import, type, ns. The body (below ---) is a single expression producing the output.

questions.q: Why should a Database Select query use input parameters (e.g. WHERE code = :code) instead of string-concatenating values into the SQL?
options: Concatenation is not supported by the Database connector
Input parameters prevent SQL injection and let the driver handle escaping and types
Input parameters make the query run in parallel
Named parameters are required for SELECT statements
questions.explanation: Parameterized queries bind values safely (:code with input parameters map #[{code: ...}]), preventing SQL injection. Concatenation works but is unsafe.

questions.q: An HTTP Request operation receives a 404 from the remote REST service. What happens by default?
options: The payload is set to null and the flow continues
A Mule error of type HTTP:NOT_FOUND is raised
The request is retried automatically
The response body is returned as the payload with no error
questions.explanation: By default only 2xx responses are treated as success; other statuses raise typed errors (HTTP:NOT_FOUND, HTTP:INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR...). The success range is configurable via the response validator / success status codes.

questions.q: Which connector is used to consume a SOAP web service in Mule 4, and how is it configured?
options: HTTP Request pointing at the WSDL
Web Service Consumer, configured with the service's WSDL location, service, port, and address
SOAPkit Router
REST Connect connector generated from the WSDL
questions.explanation: The Web Service Consumer connector consumes SOAP services from their WSDL, exposing the SOAP operations. Requests are built as XML — usually with a Transform Message before the operation.

questions.q: How are parameters typically passed to a SOAP web service operation invoked with the Web Service Consumer?
options: As query parameters on the endpoint URL
By building the expected SOAP body XML with a Transform Message (DataWeave) before the operation
As Mule variables that the connector reads automatically
In the connector's 'arguments' table
questions.explanation: The Web Service Consumer expects the payload to be the operation's SOAP body. Studio exposes the operation's metadata so a Transform Message can map data to the correct XML structure.

questions.q: A flow must process each new CSV file that appears in a directory, then prevent the same file from being processed again. Which approach is correct?
options: An On New or Updated File listener with a post-processing action (move or delete the file)
A Scheduler that reads all files every minute with no state
An HTTP Listener that watches the file system
A For Each scope around a File Read
questions.explanation: The File listener triggers per new/updated file; configuring post-processing (move to another directory, rename, or delete) prevents reprocessing. Without it, files are picked up repeatedly.

questions.q: What messaging model does a JMS queue provide, in contrast to a JMS topic?
options: Queues broadcast to all consumers; topics deliver to exactly one
Queues provide point-to-point delivery (one consumer gets each message); topics provide publish/subscribe (all subscribers get each message)
Queues are synchronous HTTP; topics are asynchronous
There is no difference in Mule 4
questions.explanation: Point-to-point (queue): each message is consumed by a single receiver. Pub/sub (topic): every active subscriber receives a copy of each message.

questions.q: What is the payload after a For Each scope finishes processing a collection?
options: The last element processed
An array of the results of each iteration
The original payload that entered the scope
null
questions.explanation: For Each returns the ORIGINAL payload; payload modifications inside the scope are discarded after it completes. Variables set inside, however, persist.

questions.q: In which Batch Job phase does the payload become a BatchJobResult object with counts of successful and failed records?
options: Load and Dispatch
Process
On Complete
Aggregate
questions.explanation: On Complete runs once after all records are processed; its payload is the BatchJobResult (processed/successful/failed counts). The original input payload is not available there.

questions.q: How are records processed inside the Process phase of a Batch Job?
options: Strictly sequentially in input order
Asynchronously and in parallel across batch steps
Only one record at a time per application
In reverse order
questions.explanation: Batch queues each record and processes them in parallel through the steps — a key difference from For Each (sequential). Order of completion is not guaranteed.

questions.q: A batch step must insert records into a database in bulk groups of 100. What component achieves this?
options: A For Each with batch size 100
A Batch Aggregator inside the batch step with size 100
A Scatter-Gather with 100 routes
The On Complete phase
questions.explanation: The Batch Aggregator collects records within a step into groups (fixed size or streaming) so they can be sent to a target in bulk — e.g. a bulk insert of 100 records.

questions.q: By default (maxFailedRecords = 0), what happens when a record fails inside a Batch Job's Process phase?
options: The record is retried until it succeeds
The job stops processing further records and moves to On Complete
The failure is silently ignored
The whole application shuts down
questions.explanation: With maxFailedRecords = 0, the first failed record halts the job. Setting it to -1 allows the job to continue regardless of failures; subsequent steps can filter on failed records with accept policies.

questions.q: A flow synchronizes new database rows every hour and must not re-process rows it has already seen, based on their incrementing ID. Which built-in mechanism fits best?
options: A Choice router comparing IDs
Automatic watermarking on the On Table Row listener using the ID as the watermark column
Storing the whole table in a variable
The Cache scope
questions.explanation: The Database connector's On Table Row source supports automatic watermarking: it stores the highest seen value of the watermark column and only returns newer rows on each poll.

questions.q: When is MANUAL watermarking (using an Object Store) required instead of automatic watermarking?
options: Whenever a Scheduler is used, because Schedulers reset automatic watermarks
When the synchronization logic needs a custom marker the source doesn't manage, e.g. comparing a max updated-at value retrieved and stored explicitly each run
Manual watermarking is never required in Mule 4
Only when using the File connector
questions.explanation: Automatic watermarking exists only on certain listener sources. When polling with a Scheduler or using custom criteria, you store/retrieve the watermark yourself with Object Store operations.

questions.q: While debugging in Anypoint Studio with a breakpoint on a processor, what can the developer inspect when execution pauses?
options: Only the payload
The full Mule event before that processor executes: payload, attributes, and variables — and evaluate DataWeave expressions
Only variables set in the current flow
The compiled Java bytecode
questions.explanation: The Mule debugger shows the entire event as it arrives at the breakpointed processor and includes an expression evaluator for ad-hoc DataWeave.

questions.q: A Mule project fails to build with 'Could not resolve dependencies ... mysql-connector-java'. What is the appropriate fix?
options: Reinstall Anypoint Studio
Add (or correct) the JDBC driver dependency in the project's pom.xml so Maven can resolve it
Copy the JAR into src/main/mule
Disable Maven in project preferences
questions.explanation: Mule apps are Maven projects; external libraries like database drivers must be declared as dependencies in pom.xml (Studio can add them via the connector config UI).

questions.q: A log shows: 'ERROR ... Message: HTTP GET on resource http://acme/api/flights failed: not found (404). Error type: HTTP:NOT_FOUND'. What is the most direct conclusion?
options: The Mule application failed to start
An outbound HTTP Request in the flow received a 404 from the remote service
The HTTP Listener rejected an incoming request
DataWeave failed to transform the payload
questions.explanation: The error type HTTP:NOT_FOUND with an outbound URL indicates the app's own HTTP Request call got a 404 from the remote endpoint — start troubleshooting at that request's URL/path.

questions.q: On which port does the Anypoint Studio embedded Mule debugger listen by default?
questions.explanation: The Mule debugger uses port 6666 by default (configurable in the run configuration). 8081 is the conventional HTTP Listener port.

questions.q: A Logger set to #[payload] prints something like 'org.mule.runtime...ManagedCursorStreamProvider'. Why?
options: The payload is null
The payload is a stream; logging shows the stream object unless it is consumed/converted (e.g. write it as a String)
The Logger is misconfigured to DEBUG level
The payload is encrypted
questions.explanation: Many connectors return streaming payloads. Logging the raw stream shows the cursor provider object; convert first (e.g. #[payload as String] or output application/json in a transform) to log content.

questions.q: What artifact is produced when a Mule 4 application is packaged for deployment?
options: A WAR file
A deployable JAR file containing the application and its dependencies
A ZIP of the Studio workspace
A Docker image (always)
questions.explanation: Mule 4 apps package as deployable JARs (mvn package or Studio export). The same artifact can be deployed to CloudHub, Runtime Fabric, or customer-hosted runtimes.

questions.q: Which statement about CloudHub workers is correct?
options: All applications in an organization share one worker
A worker is a dedicated instance of Mule hosting one application; you can scale worker size (vCores) or the number of workers
Workers are only available for APIs managed with autodiscovery
Worker size cannot be changed after deployment
questions.explanation: Each CloudHub app runs on one or more dedicated workers. Vertical scaling changes worker size; horizontal scaling adds workers (with built-in load balancing).

questions.q: What is required for API Manager policies to be enforced INSIDE a Mule application (without a separate proxy)?
options: Nothing; policies always apply automatically
An API Autodiscovery global element in the app whose API ID matches the API instance in API Manager, plus platform client credentials
A CloudHub dedicated load balancer
The APIkit Console must be enabled
questions.explanation: Autodiscovery pairs the running app with the API instance in API Manager (api id + environment client id/secret). The app then downloads and enforces the policies itself.

questions.q: A team wants to enforce rate limiting per consumer with different limits for Gold and Silver customers. What must be configured?
options: A rate limiting policy only
SLA tiers in API Manager plus an SLA-based rate limiting policy, which requires client ID enforcement so each consumer is identified
A spike control policy on the load balancer
One API instance per customer
questions.explanation: SLA-based rate limiting applies per-tier limits. Consumers register client applications, request access at a tier, and send client_id/client_secret so the policy can identify their tier.

questions.q: What is an API proxy in Anypoint Platform?
options: A DNS alias for the implementation URL
A separate application deployed by API Manager that sits in front of the implementation and enforces policies
A mock service generated from RAML
A CloudHub VPN
questions.explanation: A proxy is an auto-generated app that receives consumer traffic, applies policies (rate limiting, client enforcement...), and forwards allowed requests to the implementation — governance without touching implementation code.

questions.q: After a client ID enforcement policy is applied, what must consumers do to call the API successfully?
options: Nothing; the policy only logs usage
Request access to the API in Exchange to obtain client credentials, then send client_id/client_secret with each request as specified by the policy
Install a certificate on their machine
Use the mocking service endpoint instead
questions.explanation: Client ID enforcement rejects requests without valid credentials. Consumers register a client application (request access in Exchange), get approved, and pass the credentials in headers or query params per the policy configuration.

questions.q: Which activities are performed in Anypoint Exchange? (Select all that apply)
options: Discovering reusable API specifications and connectors
Requesting access to an API with a client application
Applying rate-limiting policies to an API instance
Viewing auto-generated API documentation and examples
questions.explanation: Exchange is the catalog: discovery, documentation/portals, and consumer access requests (creating contracts) all happen there. Applying policies to an API instance is done in API Manager.

questions.q: In RAML, what is the difference between a trait and a resourceType?
options: Traits are applied to resources; resourceTypes are applied to methods
Traits define reusable METHOD-level patterns (applied with 'is:'); resourceTypes define reusable RESOURCE-level patterns (applied with 'type:')
Traits can only hold data type definitions
There is no difference
questions.explanation: A trait captures method-level reuse (pagination params, common headers) and is applied with 'is:'. A resourceType captures a whole resource shape (e.g., collection/member) and is applied with 'type:'.

questions.q: A RAML spec declares 'baseUri: http://api.acme.com/v1' and a resource '/flights' with a nested '/{ID}'. Which URL retrieves flight 42?
options: http://api.acme.com/flights/v1/42
http://api.acme.com/v1/flights/42
http://api.acme.com/v1/flights?ID=42
http://api.acme.com/v1/{ID}/flights/42
questions.explanation: Nested resources append to their parent path under the baseUri: baseUri + /flights + /42. The {ID} is a URI parameter, filled with the actual value — not a query parameter.

questions.q: A flow sets var 'total' then calls an HTTP Request WITHOUT a target. Which statements are true afterwards? (Select all that apply)
options: The payload is the HTTP response body
vars.total is unchanged and still accessible
The attributes now contain the HTTP response status code and headers
The original request attributes are still available in attributes
questions.explanation: A connector operation replaces the message (payload AND attributes) with the operation result, but variables survive for the rest of the flow. The original attributes are gone — save them to a var if needed later.

questions.q: Which DataWeave expression reads the value of the query parameter 'code' from a request to http://host/api/flights?code=ER38sd
options: #[payload.code]
#[attributes.queryParams.code]
#[attributes.uriParams.code]
#[vars.code]
questions.explanation: Query string parameters arrive in attributes.queryParams. uriParams holds template parameters from the path (like /flights/{code}); payload is the request body.

questions.q: A property is defined as 'db.port: 3306' in the config file packaged in the JAR, and the app is deployed with the system property -Ddb.port=3307. What value does ${db.port} resolve to?
options: 3306 — files beat system properties
3307 — system/deployment properties override packaged property files
Neither; the deployment fails with a conflict
Both, depending on the flow
questions.explanation: Property precedence puts deployment and system properties above property files — that's exactly how the same JAR is configured differently per environment without rebuilding.

questions.q: Which characteristics apply to a SUBFLOW? (Select all that apply)
options: It has no event source
It has no error handling scope of its own
It can be called from a DataWeave lookup() function
Changes it makes to the event are returned to the caller
questions.explanation: Subflows have neither a source nor their own error handling (they inherit the caller's), and like any Flow Reference call, event changes flow back to the caller. lookup() can only invoke flows, not subflows.

questions.q: After scaffolding, a developer renames the APIkit-generated flow 'get:\flights:api-config' to 'getFlights'. What happens to GET /api/flights requests?
options: They still work; APIkit tracks flows internally
The router can no longer find the implementation flow — the naming convention IS the routing contract
They are routed to the console flow
They return 400 Bad Request
questions.explanation: The APIkit Router maps requests to flows BY NAME (method:\resource:config). Renaming breaks the mapping. The result surfaces as APIKIT:NOT_IMPLEMENTED (501) for that resource.

questions.q: A client sends GET /api/flights?destination=XXX where the RAML declares destination as an enum of [SFO, LAX, CLE]. What does the APIkit router do?
options: Forwards the request; validation is the flow's job
Rejects it with APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST mapped to a 400 response
Returns 404 Not Found
Strips the invalid parameter and continues
questions.explanation: The router validates requests against the spec — an out-of-enum query parameter violates the contract, raising APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST which the generated error handler maps to HTTP 400.

questions.q: A Scatter-Gather has 3 routes taking 100 ms, 200 ms, and 300 ms. Roughly how long does the router take, and what is the payload afterwards?
options: 600 ms; the last route's payload
300 ms; an object with keys '0','1','2', each holding that route's Mule message
300 ms; an array of three payloads
100 ms; the first completed route's payload
questions.explanation: Routes run concurrently, so total ≈ slowest (300 ms). The result is an object keyed by route index where each entry contains the route's full message (attributes + payload) — hence the common 'pluck $.payload' aggregation.

questions.q: Which Validation module behavior is correct?
options: Validators return true/false for use in Choice routers
Validators throw a typed error (VALIDATION namespace) when the check fails, and do nothing when it passes
Validators fix invalid data automatically
Validators only work inside APIkit flows
questions.explanation: Validation components are 'fail fast': on failure they raise e.g. VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL (catchable by error handlers); on success the event continues unchanged. They don't return booleans.

questions.q: An error handler contains, in order: On Error Continue for MULE:ANY, then On Error Propagate for HTTP:NOT_FOUND. An HTTP:NOT_FOUND error occurs. Which scope handles it?
options: The On Error Propagate — it is more specific
The On Error Continue — scopes are evaluated in order and MULE:ANY matches everything first
Both scopes execute
Neither; the error escapes
questions.explanation: On Error scopes are checked top to bottom, FIRST match wins. MULE:ANY placed first swallows every error, making later scopes unreachable — order specific types before general ones.

questions.q: Flow A (with On Error Propagate) is called via Flow Reference from Flow B. A processor in A fails. What happens in Flow B?
options: Nothing; A's handler dealt with it
The error resurfaces in B at the Flow Reference, triggering B's error handling
B continues with A's last successful payload
B restarts from its event source
questions.explanation: On Error Propagate re-throws after running its processors. To the caller, the Flow Reference itself fails — B's error handling (or the default handler) takes over. On Error Continue in A would have let B continue normally.

questions.q: Which fields exist on the Mule error object? (Select all that apply)
options: error.errorType
error.description
error.cause
error.statusCode
questions.explanation: The error object carries errorType (namespace:identifier), description, cause (underlying exception), plus errorMessage/childErrors in some cases. HTTP status codes live in error.errorMessage.attributes for HTTP errors — there is no error.statusCode field.

questions.q:
What does this script output for payload [{price: 200}, {price: 900}, {price: 500}]?

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload filter ($.price > 300) map ($.price * 2)
questions.explanation: filter keeps prices > 300 → [{price:900},{price:500}]; then map projects price*2 for each → [1800, 1000]. Note map returns what the lambda returns — here numbers, not objects.

questions.q: What is the result type of 'payload groupBy $.destination' when payload is an array of flight objects?
options: An array of arrays
An object whose keys are the destination values and whose values are arrays of the matching flights
A single object per destination
A number
questions.explanation: groupBy returns an object keyed by the grouping criteria; each key maps to the array of elements sharing that key. To get arrays back, follow with pluck or mapObject.

questions.q: Which DataWeave expression converts the string '25/12/2025' into the string '2025-12-25'?
options: ('25/12/2025' as Date {format: 'dd/MM/yyyy'}) as String {format: 'yyyy-MM-dd'}
'25/12/2025' as String {format: 'yyyy-MM-dd'}
questions.explanation: Reformatting a non-ISO date string is a two-step coercion: PARSE with the input format (as Date {format:...}), then FORMAT with the output format (as String {format:...}). A bare 'as Date' fails because the string isn't ISO-8601.

questions.q: A Transform must output XML from this input: {items: [{id: 1}, {id: 2}]}. Why does 'output application/xml --- payload' fail?
options: XML cannot represent numbers
XML requires a single root element and the array of repeated elements must be wrapped with {( ... )} inside it
DataWeave cannot output XML
The input must be XML too
questions.explanation: XML documents need exactly one root. Arrays must be converted to repeated elements, typically: items: {(payload.items map item: {id: $.id})} — the {( )} syntax spreads the array as repeated keys.

questions.q: In a DataWeave map lambda, what do $ and $$ represent?
options: $ = index, $$ = value
$ = value (current element), $$ = index
$ = payload, $$ = attributes
$ = key, $$ = namespace
questions.explanation: For arrays: $ is the current item, $$ is its index. (For object functions like mapObject/pluck: $ is the value, $$ the key.) Mixing these up is the most common DataWeave reading error.

questions.q: Why should a Database SELECT use input parameters (:param) instead of string-concatenating values into the SQL?
options: Concatenation is slower to type
Input parameters prevent SQL injection and handle type conversion/escaping safely
Input parameters allow multiple databases
The Database connector rejects concatenated strings
questions.explanation: Parameterized queries separate SQL from data, closing the injection hole and letting the driver handle typing/escaping. The connector accepts concatenated SQL — it's just dangerous.

questions.q: Which statement about the Web Service Consumer connector is TRUE?
options: It generates REST endpoints from a WSDL
Configured with a WSDL, it exposes the service's SOAP operations, and a Transform before Consume shows the expected SOAP body structure as metadata
It requires writing the SOAP envelope by hand including namespaces
It only supports one-way operations
questions.explanation: WSC reads the WSDL (service/port/address) and publishes operation metadata via DataSense, so the Transform Message before the Consume operation shows exactly the XML body to build. The envelope itself is handled by the connector.

questions.q: What payload does the On Complete phase of a Batch Job receive?
options: The original payload that triggered the job
A BatchJobResult object with counts: totalRecords, successfulRecords, failedRecords, etc.
An array of all processed records
The last record processed
questions.explanation: On Complete runs once after all records finish and receives summary statistics only — the original payload and the records themselves are NOT available there. Use it for reporting/logging.

questions.q: A Scheduler-triggered flow must fetch only records changed since its last run, across app restarts. Which components implement a manual watermark? (Select all that apply)
options: Object Store 'Retrieve' with a default value, before the query
Object Store 'Store' with the new maximum, after processing
A variable holding the last timestamp
An Until Successful scope around the query
questions.explanation: The watermark must SURVIVE restarts, so it lives in an Object Store: retrieve (with default for the first run) → query 'since' → store the new max. A var is lost when the app stops; Until Successful is retry logic, unrelated.

questions.q: In the Studio debugger, a breakpoint is set on a Transform Message. When execution pauses, what does the debugger show?
options: The event AFTER the transform executed
The event AS IT ARRIVES at the transform (before it executes), with payload, attributes, and vars inspectable
Only the payload
The compiled Java bytecode
questions.explanation: Breakpoints pause BEFORE the selected processor runs — you inspect the incoming event and can evaluate DataWeave expressions against it. Step over to see the result.

questions.q: An API instance in API Manager has SLA tiers and the SLA-based rate limiting policy applied. What must a consumer do to call the API successfully?
options: Nothing; the policy only monitors traffic
Request access via Exchange with a client application and an approved tier, then send its client_id/client_secret credentials on each request
Whitelist their IP address
Deploy their own copy of the API
questions.explanation: SLA-based policies identify consumers by client credentials issued through the Exchange access-request flow (client app + tier + approved contract). Requests without valid credentials get 401; over-tier-limit requests get 429.

sections.title: Explaining Application Network Basics
objectives: Explain MuleSoft's proposal for closing the IT delivery gap
Describe the characteristics and benefits of modern APIs and API-led connectivity
Describe the role of a Center for Enablement (C4E)
Define the basic elements of HTTP requests and responses
Describe the capabilities and high-level components of Anypoint Platform
sections.notes:
The IT delivery gap

Business demand on IT grows every year (new channels, SaaS apps, mobile, IoT), but IT delivery capacity stays roughly flat when every project is a custom point-to-point integration. Point-to-point creates tight coupling: every new connection is bespoke code, changes ripple unpredictably, and nothing is reusable. The gap between what the business asks for and what IT can deliver keeps widening.

MuleSoft's proposal: build an application network — a network of applications, data, and devices connected with reusable, discoverable, self-serve APIs. Key properties of an application network:

  • It is built incrementally, project by project ("bendable, not breakable"). Each project consumes existing APIs and contributes new ones, so delivery gets faster over time.
  • Nodes are loosely coupled: consumers depend on API contracts, not implementations, so implementations can change without breaking consumers.
  • Assets are discoverable and self-serve through Anypoint Exchange — teams find and reuse instead of rebuilding.
  • It is recomposable: existing APIs are rewired into new products and channels quickly.

API-led connectivity — the three layers

  • System APIs — mediate access to a core system (one system, canonical data model). They hide complexity (SOAP, JDBC, proprietary formats) and shield consumers from system changes/migrations.
  • Process APIs — encapsulate business processes that interact with and shape data across systems (e.g. "create order" touching CRM + ERP). They are independent of both the source systems and the delivery channels.
  • Experience APIs — reconfigure data so it is easily consumed by its intended audience/channel, without duplicating process logic per channel.

Exam tip: given a scenario ("an API that aggregates customer data from three systems for a mobile app"), be able to place each API in the correct layer. Aggregation/orchestration → Process; channel-specific shaping → Experience; direct system unlock → System.

Modern APIs

MuleSoft describes modern APIs as having three characteristics: they are treated as products (designed for and marketed to consumers, with a lifecycle), they are discoverable and self-describing (documentation, specs, examples in Exchange), and they are managed (secured, monitored, governed with policies and SLAs).

Center for Enablement (C4E)

A C4E is a cross-functional team (IT + line-of-business members) that enables the rest of the organization instead of delivering everything itself:

  • Builds and publishes reusable assets (APIs, templates, examples, best-practice guides) as products.
  • Establishes and evangelizes best practices; supports and trains project teams.
  • Measures consumption of assets (KPIs like reuse rate) — the network's value grows with reuse.

Contrast with a traditional Center of Excellence (CoE): a CoE centralizes delivery ("do it for the teams"), which becomes a bottleneck; a C4E decentralizes delivery and centralizes enablement.

HTTP fundamentals

MethodPurposeHas body?Idempotent?
GETRetrieve a resource/collectionNoYes (safe)
POSTCreate a resource / process dataYesNo
PUTReplace a resource at a known URI (create-or-replace)YesYes
PATCHPartially update a resourceYesNo (in general)
DELETERemove a resourceNoYes

An HTTP request = method + URI (+ query string) + protocol version + headers + optional body. An HTTP response = status line + headers + optional body. Common status codes:

CodeMeaning
200 / 201 / 202 / 204OK / Created / Accepted (async) / No Content
301 / 304Moved Permanently / Not Modified
400 / 401 / 403 / 404 / 405 / 415 / 429Bad Request / Unauthorized (not authenticated) / Forbidden (not authorized) / Not Found / Method Not Allowed / Unsupported Media Type / Too Many Requests (rate limit)
500 / 502 / 503 / 504Internal Server Error / Bad Gateway / Service Unavailable / Gateway Timeout

Anypoint Platform components

AreaComponentWhat it does
DesignDesign Center (API Designer)Web tool to write API specifications (RAML/OAS) and build fragments; includes the mocking service
Anypoint Studio / Code BuilderDesktop/cloud IDEs to implement, test, and debug Mule applications
Share & discoverAnypoint ExchangeCatalog of reusable assets: specs, fragments, connectors, templates, examples, custom policies; generates API portals and REST Connect connectors
Manage (control plane)API ManagerCreate/manage API instances, apply policies, SLA tiers, contracts, alerts, proxies
Runtime ManagerDeploy, manage, scale, and monitor applications on any deployment target
Access ManagementUsers, roles, permissions, business groups, environments, connected apps
Anypoint Monitoring / VisualizerDashboards, logs, alerts / real-time visualization of the application network
Run (runtime plane)Mule runtimeRuns apps on CloudHub (MuleSoft-hosted), Runtime Fabric (containers), or customer-hosted servers

Exam tip: know which component does what. "Where do consumers discover and request access to an API?" → Exchange. "Where are policies applied?" → API Manager. "Where do you scale workers?" → Runtime Manager.

sections.title: Designing and Consuming APIs
objectives: Describe the lifecycle of the modern API (design, simulate, feedback, validate)
Use RAML to define API resources, nested resources, and methods
Identify when to use query parameters vs URI parameters
Use RAML to define request/response bodies, headers, and status codes
Use RAML to define reusable data types and format-independent examples
Formulate RESTful requests from a RAML API specification
sections.notes:
The design-first API lifecycle

Modern APIs are designed before they are implemented: design → simulate → get feedback → validate, iterating until consumers agree the contract is right. Only then is the API implemented, tested, deployed, and managed. Tools per stage:

  • Design — API Designer (Design Center) writing RAML or OAS.
  • Simulate — the mocking service serves responses straight from the spec's examples, giving consumers a live endpoint before any code exists.
  • Feedback — publish to Exchange; consumers explore the auto-generated API console/portal and comment.
  • Validate — refine the spec until it is stable; it becomes the contract that APIkit later enforces.

RAML essentials

#%RAML 1.0
title: American Flights API
version: v1
baseUri: http://localhost:8081/api

types:
  AmericanFlight: !include dataTypes/american-flight.raml

/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      destination:
        required: false
        enum: [SFO, LAX, CLE]
    responses:
      200:
        body:
          application/json:
            type: AmericanFlight[]
            examples: !include examples/AmericanFlightsExample.raml
  post:
    body:
      application/json:
        type: AmericanFlight
    responses:
      201:
        body:
          application/json:
            example: {"message": "Flight added"}
  /{ID}:
    get:
      responses:
        200:
          body:
            application/json:
              type: AmericanFlight
    put:
    delete:
  • RAML is YAML-based: indentation defines nesting, and it is case-sensitive.
  • Resources begin with /; nesting a resource under another creates a sub-path (/flights/{ID}).
  • URI parameters use {curlyBraces} and identify a specific resource. Query parameters are declared under a method's queryParameters: and filter/sort/paginate collections. Rule of thumb: identity → URI parameter; refinement of a result set → query parameter.
  • Each method can declare headers:, body: (per media type), and responses: (per status code, each with its own body/type/example).

Reuse in RAML

MechanismScopeExample use
types: / DataType fragmentData structurestype: AmericanFlight shared by requests and responses
NamedExample fragmentExample instancesOne example file reused by several methods (format-independent)
traits: (applied with is:)Method-level patternsPagination query params, standard error responses, auth headers
resourceTypes: (applied with type:)Resource-level patternsStandard "collection" and "member" resource shapes
Library (uses:)Bundle of types/traits/etc.Common enterprise definitions shared across specs
Overlay / ExtensionWhole specAdd annotations/translations (overlay) or environment additions (extension) without touching the master spec

Fragments (#%RAML 1.0 DataType, Trait, NamedExample…) can live in separate files included with !include, or be published to Exchange as reusable assets and imported into many API specs as dependencies.

A RAML data type defines fields, types, and constraints:

#%RAML 1.0 DataType
type: object
properties:
  ID: integer
  code:
    type: string
    pattern: "[A-Z]{3}\\d{3}"
  price:
    type: number
    required: false

Consuming APIs

  • To call an API from a spec: match method + full path (baseUri + resource) + required parameters/headers/body. E.g. from the spec above: GET http://localhost:8081/api/flights?destination=SFO or POST /api/flights with a JSON AmericanFlight body.
  • When a spec is published to Exchange, REST Connect automatically generates a REST Connector (one operation per method) that can be dragged into a Mule flow — an alternative to configuring raw HTTP Request operations.
  • The API console (in Design Center or Exchange) and the mocking service let anyone try requests without an implementation.

Exam tip: expect questions that show a RAML snippet and ask which request is valid (correct path nesting, query vs URI parameter, required vs optional). Read the indentation carefully — it defines what belongs to what.

sections.title: Accessing and Modifying Mule Events
objectives: Describe the Mule event data structure (message payload, attributes, variables)
Use transformers to set the payload, attributes, and variables
Write DataWeave expressions to access and modify payload, attributes, and variables
Enrich Mule events using target parameters
sections.notes:
Anatomy of a Mule event

  • Payload — the body/data being processed. Can be any type: JSON, XML, Java object, stream…
  • Attributes — metadata that arrived with the message. For an HTTP Listener: attributes.queryParams, attributes.uriParams, attributes.headers, attributes.method, attributes.requestPath. For a File listener: file name, size, timestamps.
  • Variables (vars) — data you attach to the event; they live for the rest of the flow and travel through Flow References.

The Mule event is immutable — every change creates a new event instance behind the scenes. Practically, this means each processor receives the event produced by the previous one.

The golden rule of attributes

Every time the event passes through a connector operation (HTTP Request, Database Select, File Read…), the message is replaced: the payload becomes the operation's result AND the attributes are replaced with metadata about that result (e.g. HTTP response status and headers). If you need the original query params later, save them to a variable first.

Accessing event data with DataWeave

payload                              // whole payload
payload.customer.lastName            // field navigation
payload[0].price                     // array index
attributes.queryParams.destination   // ?destination=SFO
attributes.uriParams.ID              // /flights/{ID}
attributes.headers.'client_id'       // request header
vars.myVar                           // a variable
vars.order.items[0]                  // variables hold any structure
correlationId                        // event correlation ID
message                              // the whole message (payload+attributes)

Inline expressions inside component fields use #[ ... ], e.g. a Logger message of #['Got flight ' ++ attributes.uriParams.ID]. Property placeholders (${http.port}) are resolved at startup and are NOT DataWeave.

Setting event data

ComponentSetsNotes
Transform Messagepayload, any variable, attributesFull DataWeave script per output; the primary transformation tool
Set PayloadpayloadValue field accepts literals or #[expressions]; can set MIME type
Set Variableone variableName + value (literal or expression)
Remove Variable—Deletes a variable from the event

Enrichment with target parameters

Most connector operations expose target and target value parameters. Setting target = flightData (and optionally targetValue = #[payload.data]) stores the operation's result in vars.flightData and leaves the current payload untouched — the Mule 4 replacement for Mule 3's message enricher. Use it whenever you must combine "what I have" with "what another system returns".

Exam tip: after an HTTP Request with target=response, the payload is unchanged and vars.response holds the response body. Without a target, payload = response body and attributes = response metadata. Both facts are heavily tested.

sections.title: Structuring Mule Applications
objectives: Parameterize an application using property placeholders
Define and reuse global configurations in an application
Break an application into multiple flows using flow references, private flows, and subflows
Specify what data is persisted between flows and across a flow reference
Describe the purpose of domain projects
sections.notes:
Project structure

my-app/
├── pom.xml                     <- Maven build (dependencies, plugins)
├── mule-artifact.json          <- app descriptor (minMuleVersion, secureProperties)
└── src/
    ├── main/
    │   ├── mule/               <- Mule configuration XMLs (flows, global elements)
    │   └── resources/          <- properties files, DW modules, examples, log4j2.xml
    └── test/
        ├── munit/              <- MUnit test suites
        └── resources/

Property placeholders

Externalize anything environment-specific (hosts, ports, credentials, paths):

# src/main/resources/config/dev.yaml
db:
  host: "dev-db.acme.com"
  port: "3306"
http:
  listener:
    port: "8081"
  • Register the file with a Configuration properties global element. Parameterize the file name itself to switch environments: config/${env}.yaml, then run with -Denv=dev (in Studio: Run Configuration → VM arguments -Denv=dev -M-Denv=dev pattern for Maven).
  • Reference values as ${db.host} in any configuration field, or p('db.host') inside DataWeave.
  • Precedence: deployment/system properties (-D, Runtime Manager properties) override file values — this is how the same JAR runs in every environment.
  • Secrets should use secure configuration properties (encrypted) — covered in depth in MCD L2, but know they exist and use the secure:: prefix.

Global configurations

Connector configurations (HTTP Listener config, HTTP Request config, Database config…) are global elements: defined once, referenced by any number of operations. Best practice is to keep them in a dedicated config file (e.g. global.xml) so they're easy to find and reuse across the app's XML files — all files are combined at deployment.

Flows, private flows, subflows

FlowPrivate flowSubflow
Event sourceYes (optional but typical)NoNo
Own error handlingYesYesNo — inherits caller's
Invoked bySource or Flow ReferenceFlow ReferenceFlow Reference
Performance——Slightly faster (no extra context)
Callable from DataWeave lookup()YesYesNo

A Flow Reference passes the entire event (payload + attributes + variables) into the referenced flow synchronously. Changes made there (payload, new variables) return to the caller. This is fundamentally different from crossing a transport boundary:

  • App A calls App B over HTTP → only the payload (and headers you set) cross; variables and attributes do NOT.
  • App B receives a brand-new event with its own attributes (from B's HTTP Listener) and empty vars.

Exam tip: "A flow sets var X then calls another flow in the same app via Flow Reference — is X visible?" YES. "…calls another Mule app via HTTP Request — is X visible there?" NO.

Async scope

The Async scope runs its processors in a separate thread and the flow continues immediately — use it for fire-and-forget work (audit logging, notifications) that shouldn't delay the response. The async branch gets a copy of the event; its changes don't come back.

Domain projects

A Mule domain is a special project that holds shared global configurations. Applications deployed to the same customer-hosted runtime can reference one domain and share, e.g., a single HTTP Listener config — meaning several apps share one port. Domains reduce duplicated config and resource usage, but: not supported on CloudHub (each CloudHub app runs isolated on its own worker), all apps must be deployed alongside the domain, and redeploying the domain restarts its apps.

sections.title: Building API Implementation Interfaces
objectives: Manually create a RESTful interface for a Mule application
Generate a REST Connector from a RAML specification
Describe the features and benefits of APIkit
Use APIkit to create implementation flows from a RAML file
Describe how requests are routed through flows generated by APIkit
sections.notes:
Manual RESTful interfaces

You can hand-build an interface: one HTTP Listener per resource/method (paths like /flights/{ID}, listener metadata gives attributes.uriParams), or one listener with wildcard path (/*) plus Choice routers on attributes.requestPath and attributes.method. It works, but you must implement validation, error responses, and routing yourself — and nothing guarantees you stay in sync with the API specification. That is the problem APIkit solves.

APIkit

APIkit is an open-source toolkit that scaffolds a Mule application from an API specification (RAML or OAS; newer versions also support AsyncAPI, GraphQL, OData, SOAP via APIkit for SOAP). Import the spec from Exchange or the filesystem into a Mule project, and scaffolding generates:

  • The main flow: HTTP Listener (default path /api/*) → APIkit Router, plus an error handler with mappings for the standard APIkit errors.
  • One implementation flow per resource/method pair, named by strict convention: get:\flights:american-flights-api-config, get:\flights\(ID):american-flights-api-config, post:\flights:application\json:american-flights-api-config (the media type appears when a request body is declared). The flow name is the routing contract — never rename it.
  • A console flow exposing the interactive API console (default path /console/*).

How the APIkit Router works

  1. Validates each incoming request against the specification: path exists, method allowed, required query params/headers present, body matches the declared type.
  2. Routes valid requests to the implementation flow whose name matches the method + resource (+ media type) + router config name.
  3. Populates attributes.uriParams and attributes.queryParams.
  4. Serializes the flow's output as the HTTP response; sets the status code (200 by default, or whatever the flow sets in attributes / the listener's response builder).

Validation failures raise typed errors mapped by the generated error handler:

APIkit errorHTTP status
APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST400
APIKIT:NOT_FOUND404
APIKIT:METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED405
APIKIT:NOT_ACCEPTABLE406
APIKIT:UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE415
APIKIT:NOT_IMPLEMENTED501

Exam tip: a request for a resource that exists in the spec but has no implementation flow yet → the scaffolded flow returns the spec's example (if scaffolded with examples) — while a path that isn't in the spec at all → APIKIT:NOT_FOUND → 404. A missing required query param → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST → 400.

Keeping spec and implementation in sync

  • Update the RAML → right-click the API in Studio → Scaffold flows from these API specifications: new resource/method pairs get new flows; existing implementation flows are preserved.
  • The spec can be kept as a design library dependency from Exchange, so implementation always tracks the published contract version.

REST Connect connectors

When an API specification is published to Exchange, the platform auto-generates a REST Connector (REST Connect). Consumers add it from Exchange to their Mule project and get one typed operation per method — with fields for parameters and metadata for DataSense — instead of hand-configuring HTTP Request URLs. If the connector can't be generated, consumers can still download the spec or call the API with plain HTTP Request.

sections.title: Routing Events
objectives: Use the Choice router to route events based on conditional logic
Use the Scatter-Gather router to multicast events
Validate data using the Validation module
sections.notes:
Choice router — conditional, exactly one route

<choice>
  <when expression="#[payload.destination == 'SFO']">
    ... route 1 ...
  </when>
  <when expression="#[payload.destination == 'LAX']">
    ... route 2 ...
  </when>
  <otherwise>
    ... default route ...
  </otherwise>
</choice>
  • Routes are evaluated top to bottom; the first expression returning true wins — order your conditions from most to least specific.
  • If nothing matches and there is no otherwise, the event continues unchanged past the router.
  • Equivalent to if / else-if / else. For value-based transformation, a DataWeave if/else or match inside a Transform is often cleaner than a Choice.

Scatter-Gather — concurrent multicast

  • Sends a copy of the event to every route concurrently (minimum two routes) and waits for all to finish (configurable timeout).
  • The result is a new payload: an object keyed by route index, each entry holding that route's full Mule message:
{
  "0": { "attributes": {...}, "payload": [ ...route 0 result... ] },
  "1": { "attributes": {...}, "payload": [ ...route 1 result... ] }
}

Typical aggregation after the router:

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
flatten(payload pluck $.payload)   // merge all route payloads into one array
  • Error behavior: all routes still run; if any fail, the router raises MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING aggregating each route's error. To tolerate partial failure, put a Try scope (with On Error Continue) inside the failing-prone routes.
  • Use when independent calls can run in parallel (e.g. query three airline systems and combine results) — total time ≈ slowest route, not the sum.

Other routers (know they exist)

RouterBehavior
First SuccessfulTries routes in order until one completes without error (failover pattern)
Round RobinOne route per event, rotating through routes (simple load distribution)

Validation module

Validators check a condition and, on failure, throw a Mule error in the VALIDATION namespace (they don't return booleans). This "fail fast with a typed error" style pairs naturally with error handlers and APIkit (map to 400 responses).

  • Common validators: Is true / Is false, Is not blank string / Is blank string, Is not null / Is null, Is number, Is email, Is IP, Matches regex, Is elapsed, Is not elapsed.
  • Each validator lets you customize the error message; the raised type is specific, e.g. VALIDATION:INVALID_BOOLEAN, VALIDATION:BLANK_STRING, VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL.
  • All / Any validators combine several validations into one result.

Exam tip: "Route a copy of the message to ALL of these systems" → Scatter-Gather. "Route to ONE system depending on a field" → Choice. "Reject requests with an invalid email, returning a clear error" → Validation + error handler. Also remember Scatter-Gather's output shape — questions love asking what the payload looks like afterwards.

sections.title: Handling Errors
objectives: Describe the default error handling in a Mule application
Define a custom global default error handler and identify when it is used
Compare and contrast On Error Continue and On Error Propagate scopes
Handle errors at the application, flow, and processor level
Describe the data structure of the Mule Error object
Map errors to custom application errors
sections.notes:
Two kinds of errors

  • System errors — occur outside event processing (app startup failure, a connection lost while idle). No Mule event is involved, so no error handler can catch them; Mule logs them and applies reconnection strategies.
  • Messaging errors — thrown while an event is processed in a flow. These are what you handle with error handlers.

The Mule Error object

When a processor fails, execution jumps to error handling and the event gains an error object:

FieldContents
error.errorTypeNamespace + identifier, e.g. HTTP:NOT_FOUND, VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL
error.descriptionHuman-readable description of what failed
error.causeThe underlying Java exception
error.errorMessageFor some connectors, a Mule message describing the failing response (e.g. the HTTP error body)
error.childErrorsAggregated errors (e.g. from Scatter-Gather composite routing)

Error types form a hierarchy. Every error extends MULE:ANY. Examples: HTTP:NOT_FOUND and HTTP:CONNECTIVITY are HTTP-namespace types; HTTP:CONNECTIVITY also extends MULE:CONNECTIVITY. Handling a parent type catches all of its children. Special types: MULE:UNKNOWN (unmatchable except by ANY), MULE:CRITICAL (not handleable at all).

Default behavior (no error handler anywhere)

Mule applies the built-in default error handler, which propagates the error: it is logged, the flow stops, and the source gets the error response — an HTTP Listener returns 500 with the error description.

On Error Propagate vs On Error Continue

On Error PropagateOn Error Continue
After handler runsRe-throws the error upContinues as if the flow succeeded
Rest of the flowSkippedSkipped (handler output becomes the flow result)
HTTP Listener returnsError response (500 by default) — body can be customized in the handlerSuccess response (200) with the handler's payload
When called via Flow ReferenceError surfaces in the calling flow at the Flow ReferenceCaller continues normally after the Flow Reference

Each On Error scope can match on: type (one or more error types, comma-separated) and/or when (a DataWeave boolean, e.g. #[error.errorType.identifier == 'NOT_FOUND']). Within an error handler, scopes are checked in order; first match wins — put specific types before general ones. An unmatched error behaves as if the handler didn't exist (bubbles to the next level).

Three levels of handling

  1. Processor level — Try scope. Wrap one or more processors; attach its own error handler. Classic use: let one optional call fail (On Error Continue in the Try) while the rest of the flow proceeds.
  2. Flow level. Each flow (and private flow) has an error handling section with its own On Error scopes.
  3. Application level — global default error handler. Define a global error-handler element and set it as the app's default in the configuration (Global Elements → Configuration → Default Error Handler). It applies only to flows that define no error handling of their own — it's a fallback, never an addition. Subflows never have their own handling; they always use the caller's context.

Raising and mapping errors

  • Raise Error component throws a custom error explicitly: type like APP:ORDER_INVALID plus a description — useful after a business rule check.
  • Error mapping (on any connector operation, "Error Mapping" tab) converts a source type into a custom one: e.g. map HTTP:NOT_FOUND from a customer-service call to APP:CUSTOMER_NOT_FOUND.
